query: What is an alternative mode of assessment and how do I apply for one?

queries: What is an alternative mode of assessment and how do I apply for one?

retrieved.title: Alternative mode of assessment
retrieved.text:
Alternative modes of assessment allow for exams that

- cover the same contents;
- require the same performance;
- but are held in a different/flexible mode.

The aim is to design exams and courses in such a way that affected students do not experience disadvantages due to their impairment.

An alternative mode of assessment does not mean that

- the affected students' achievements are not assessed;
- affected students are given an advantage;
- the examiners “turn a blind eye” or that
- studies are made easier for the affected students (“being carried through the degree programme”).

The changed mode of assessment comes closest to the initially intended mode while taking the student’s impairment into account. This is what makes studying for people with disabilities (creating more equal opportunities) possible in the first place.

Any type of exam and proof of performance can have an alternative mode of assessment. This includes exams that take place on site as well as in digital or hybrid form.

Examples of exam types where alternative examination methods can be arranged include:

- Oral exams
- Written exams
- Multiple-choice examinations
- Intermediate tests
- Presentations
- Active participation (e.g., discussions)
- Lab work
- Submissions (e.g., essays, final papers)
- Excursions, trips, museum visits, etc.

heading_path: Procedure for claiming an alternative mode of assessment
retrieved.text:
Exams can be modified in two ways to meet your needs based on your impairment:

- O[ption 1](#c639477): Direct communication and an agreement with the lecturer/examiner
- O[ption 2](#c639478): Submitting an application to the directorate of studies (SPL) [JP1] and presenting this application to the lecturer/examiner

In some cases, submitting a formal application ([option 2](#c639478)) can make more sense and/or be necessary. Please discuss your situation with your teachers/examiners and/or the Accessible Studying Team or the responsible directorate of studies. Please read the regulations by the individual directorates of studies (SPL) (links below) and check whether your SPL recommends one of the two options.

Consider which alternative mode of assessment might be most useful and effective for you to outweigh the disadvantages you experience.

- You are welcome to use the counselling offers of the Accessible Studying Team to find suitable (additional) alternative methods for your situation.

1. Discuss the necessary alternative modes of assessment – as soon as possible – with your lecturers/examiners directly and agree on the practical implementation.
2. Together, record what you agreed on in writing (e.g. via e-mail to ensure that all parties involved are well-informed and that the agreement is also transparent for others in the future).
3. If you are asked to provide evidence of your impairment because it is not obvious, please read the [information about the provision of evidence](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/nachweis-der-beeintraechtigung/) (in German).

retrieved.title: Alternative mode of assessment
retrieved.text:
If your application is granted or if you received a “letter of recommendation” (issued until February 2020), your teachers/examiners are not automatically notified and you are not automatically registered in the relevant university system.

A granted application merely confirms your right to the alternative mode of assessment and provides a solid basis for communication. You have to independently contact your lecturers/examiners in all your courses/exams and inform them that you require an alternative mode of assessment. This is also due to reasons of data protection. Furthermore, some impairments occur in episodes or phases. Therefore, affected students might not have the same needs at all times. An automated system could not take this into account.

Therefore, contact your lecturer or examiner as soon as possible to discuss your needs.

According to section 59, para. 1, item 12 of the [2002 Universities Act](https://www.ris.bka.gv.at/GeltendeFassung.wxe?Abfrage=Bundesnormen&Gesetzesnummer=20002128) (in German), degree programme students and non-degree programme students with an [impairment that directly affects their studies](/en/accessible-studies/) have the right to alternative modes of assessment.

In addition, the Statutes of the University of Vienna specify that there are [equal opportunities for people with a disability and/or a chronic illness](https://satzung.univie.ac.at/en/more-parts-of-the-statutes/equal-opportunities-for-people-with-disabilities-andor-chronic-illnesses/).

You can find the detailed legal texts on the [Accessible University of Vienna portal](https://barrierefrei.univie.ac.at/weiterfuehrend/rechtliches-und-nachweise/pruefungen/) (in German).

retrieved.title: Changes to exams and teaching
retrieved.text:
Many students and prospective students with impairments are entitled to request exam adaptations or alternative modes of assessment. Read the information provided below to find out whether you are eligible, how the application and/or implementation works and what types of evidence you have to provide.

- [Exam adaptations for the entrance exam procedure (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/aufnahmeverfahren/aufnahme-eignungsverfahren-mit-beeintraechtigung/)

- [Alternative modes of assessment](/en/accessible-studies/changes-to-exams-and-teaching/alternative-mode-of-assessment/)
- [Modified curriculum (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/modifiziertes-curriculum/)
- [Discontinuing exams or courses for an important reason (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/abbruch-von-pruefungen-oder-lehrveranstaltungen-aus-wichtigem-grund/)

By changing the mode of assessment, it is possible to outweigh many disadvantages resulting from an impairment. © Universität Wien / Barbara Mair

For many adaptations and support offers, you have to provide evidence to the University of Vienna of how the impairment manifests itself and how it affects your studies. Please note the general information about types of evidence you have to present to the University of Vienna.

[Link to further information about evidence of impairment](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/nachweis-der-beeintraechtigung/)

retrieved.title: Proof of impairment
retrieved.text:
In order to access many of the offered support serivces at the University of Vienna it is required to provide proof of impairment.

Depending on your individual needs, different offices are responsible for managing your documentation. The type of proof needed may vary. Listed below you can find out which kind of documentation you need in order to access different services:

Alternative modes of assessment involve adjustments to exams/courses to compensate for disadvantages students with impairments/illnesses may face.

To apply for alternative modes of assessment, you will need:

- **a medical/clinical psychological certificate**
- or the “**[Form without Diagnosis](/fileadmin/user_upload/p_studieren/p_barrierefreistudieren/bf_docs/Formular_Besta__tigung_studienrelevante_Beeintraechtigungen_EN.pdf)**” which must be filled out by a medical provider
- a "[Disability Identity Card](https://www.oesterreich.gv.at/en/themen/menschen_mit_behinderungen/behindertenpass.html)" with up to date information about a the relevant impairment

Students that experience acute health problems or happen to be in an accident right before or during an exam or course with compulsory attendance can be signed out without any consequences.Your lecturer/examiner may ask you for documentation to validate the existence of a justified reason for signing you out of a course/exam which is already in progress.

In order to do so you can provide either:

- a  **medical/clinical psychological certificate**
- or a relevant doctor's note by your primary care provider

retrieved.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
retrieved.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

retrieved.text: ## / - Step 1: Check your record of examinations and/or semester planner You can find the record of examinations in u:space under the tab “Studies”. reranked.title: Alternative mode of assessment
reranked.text:
Alternative modes of assessment allow for exams that

- cover the same contents;
- require the same performance;
- but are held in a different/flexible mode.

The aim is to design exams and courses in such a way that affected students do not experience disadvantages due to their impairment.

An alternative mode of assessment does not mean that

- the affected students' achievements are not assessed;
- affected students are given an advantage;
- the examiners “turn a blind eye” or that
- studies are made easier for the affected students (“being carried through the degree programme”).

The changed mode of assessment comes closest to the initially intended mode while taking the student’s impairment into account. This is what makes studying for people with disabilities (creating more equal opportunities) possible in the first place.

Any type of exam and proof of performance can have an alternative mode of assessment. This includes exams that take place on site as well as in digital or hybrid form.

Examples of exam types where alternative examination methods can be arranged include:

- Oral exams
- Written exams
- Multiple-choice examinations
- Intermediate tests
- Presentations
- Active participation (e.g., discussions)
- Lab work
- Submissions (e.g., essays, final papers)
- Excursions, trips, museum visits, etc.

####

reranked.title: Alternative mode of assessment
heading_path: Procedure for claiming an alternative mode of assessment
reranked.text:
Exams can be modified in two ways to meet your needs based on your impairment:

- O[ption 1](#c639477): Direct communication and an agreement with the lecturer/examiner
- O[ption 2](#c639478): Submitting an application to the directorate of studies (SPL) [JP1] and presenting this application to the lecturer/examiner

In some cases, submitting a formal application ([option 2](#c639478)) can make more sense and/or be necessary. Please discuss your situation with your teachers/examiners and/or the Accessible Studying Team or the responsible directorate of studies. Please read the regulations by the individual directorates of studies (SPL) (links below) and check whether your SPL recommends one of the two options.

Consider which alternative mode of assessment might be most useful and effective for you to outweigh the disadvantages you experience.

- You are welcome to use the counselling offers of the Accessible Studying Team to find suitable (additional) alternative methods for your situation.

1. Discuss the necessary alternative modes of assessment – as soon as possible – with your lecturers/examiners directly and agree on the practical implementation.
2. Together, record what you agreed on in writing (e.g. via e-mail to ensure that all parties involved are well-informed and that the agreement is also transparent for others in the future).
3. If you are asked to provide evidence of your impairment because it is not obvious, please read the [information about the provision of evidence](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/nachweis-der-beeintraechtigung/) (in German).

reranked.title: Alternative mode of assessment
reranked.text:
If your application is granted or if you received a “letter of recommendation” (issued until February 2020), your teachers/examiners are not automatically notified and you are not automatically registered in the relevant university system.

A granted application merely confirms your right to the alternative mode of assessment and provides a solid basis for communication. You have to independently contact your lecturers/examiners in all your courses/exams and inform them that you require an alternative mode of assessment. This is also due to reasons of data protection. Furthermore, some impairments occur in episodes or phases. Therefore, affected students might not have the same needs at all times. An automated system could not take this into account.

Therefore, contact your lecturer or examiner as soon as possible to discuss your needs.

According to section 59, para. 1, item 12 of the [2002 Universities Act](https://www.ris.bka.gv.at/GeltendeFassung.wxe?Abfrage=Bundesnormen&Gesetzesnummer=20002128) (in German), degree programme students and non-degree programme students with an [impairment that directly affects their studies](/en/accessible-studies/) have the right to alternative modes of assessment.

In addition, the Statutes of the University of Vienna specify that there are [equal opportunities for people with a disability and/or a chronic illness](https://satzung.univie.ac.at/en/more-parts-of-the-statutes/equal-opportunities-for-people-with-disabilities-andor-chronic-illnesses/).

You can find the detailed legal texts on the [Accessible University of Vienna portal](https://barrierefrei.univie.ac.at/weiterfuehrend/rechtliches-und-nachweise/pruefungen/) (in German).

reranked.title: Changes to exams and teaching
reranked.text:
Many students and prospective students with impairments are entitled to request exam adaptations or alternative modes of assessment. Read the information provided below to find out whether you are eligible, how the application and/or implementation works and what types of evidence you have to provide.

- [Exam adaptations for the entrance exam procedure (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/aufnahmeverfahren/aufnahme-eignungsverfahren-mit-beeintraechtigung/)

- [Alternative modes of assessment](/en/accessible-studies/changes-to-exams-and-teaching/alternative-mode-of-assessment/)
- [Modified curriculum (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/modifiziertes-curriculum/)
- [Discontinuing exams or courses for an important reason (in German)](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/abbruch-von-pruefungen-oder-lehrveranstaltungen-aus-wichtigem-grund/)

By changing the mode of assessment, it is possible to outweigh many disadvantages resulting from an impairment. © Universität Wien / Barbara Mair

For many adaptations and support offers, you have to provide evidence to the University of Vienna of how the impairment manifests itself and how it affects your studies. Please note the general information about types of evidence you have to present to the University of Vienna.

[Link to further information about evidence of impairment](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/nachweis-der-beeintraechtigung/)

reranked.title: Proof of impairment
reranked.text:
In order to access many of the offered support serivces at the University of Vienna it is required to provide proof of impairment.

Depending on your individual needs, different offices are responsible for managing your documentation. The type of proof needed may vary. Listed below you can find out which kind of documentation you need in order to access different services:

Alternative modes of assessment involve adjustments to exams/courses to compensate for disadvantages students with impairments/illnesses may face.

To apply for alternative modes of assessment, you will need:

- **a medical/clinical psychological certificate**
- or the “**[Form without Diagnosis](/fileadmin/user_upload/p_studieren/p_barrierefreistudieren/bf_docs/Formular_Besta__tigung_studienrelevante_Beeintraechtigungen_EN.pdf)**” which must be filled out by a medical provider
- a "[Disability Identity Card](https://www.oesterreich.gv.at/en/themen/menschen_mit_behinderungen/behindertenpass.html)" with up to date information about a the relevant impairment

Students that experience acute health problems or happen to be in an accident right before or during an exam or course with compulsory attendance can be signed out without any consequences.Your lecturer/examiner may ask you for documentation to validate the existence of a justified reason for signing you out of a course/exam which is already in progress.

In order to do so you can provide either:

- a  **medical/clinical psychological certificate**
- or a relevant doctor's note by your primary care provider

reranked.title: AI and Exams
heading_path: Examinations with AI: What does apply here?
reranked.text: At the University of Vienna, an examination is a formal opportunity for students to demonstrate the level of knowledge and skills they have acquired and the achievement of the learning outcomes defined in the curriculum. It is not merely a matter of assessing performance for the award of a grade or academic degree, but also of receiving valuable feedback on learning progress and ensuring fairness and academic integrity. In addition to oral and written examinations, written assignments and presentations also count as examinations. In the current age of AI, the nature of examinations is evolving. The focus is shifting from mere knowledge reproduction to assessing higher levels of competence, such as reflection, transfer and design. Consequently, emphasis is now on both the final outcome and the individual learner's progress and development. Whether and how you may use AI in examinations is determined by the respective examiners. - Where can you find the rules and regulations? The binding provisions specifying which AI tools are permitted for a particular course or examination can be found directly in the course description in u:find. As a general rule, anything not explicitly listed there as permitted is prohibited. If you are unsure, you should always consult your lecturers in order to clarify expectations and framework conditions at an early stage. - Transparency and documentation: If you are permitted to use AI tools for written assignments,

If you are on the waiting list, please attend the first unit of the course as there may be free places. - Alternative place available (Alternativplatz verfügbar): You have not gained a place on the course of your interest, but will be able to attend another group (parallel course). You can accept or reject it within a defined deadline. - In process (In Bearbeitung): The registration period has expired and the allocation of places or your record is being processed. This can take several hours. In the meanwhile, you cannot make any changes. - Closed (geschlossen): At the end of the semester, all registrations are closed, which enables you to register for the same or a similar course or exam without any problems in the following semester. - **No place received** (Keinen Platz erhalten): You have been on the waiting list of a course/exam and did not receive a place. selected.title: Alternative mode of assessment
selected.text:
Alternative modes of assessment allow for exams that

- cover the same contents;
- require the same performance;
- but are held in a different/flexible mode.

The aim is to design exams and courses in such a way that affected students do not experience disadvantages due to their impairment.

An alternative mode of assessment does not mean that

- the affected students' achievements are not assessed;
- affected students are given an advantage;
- the examiners “turn a blind eye” or that
- studies are made easier for the affected students (“being carried through the degree programme”).

The changed mode of assessment comes closest to the initially intended mode while taking the student’s impairment into account. This is what makes studying for people with disabilities (creating more equal opportunities) possible in the first place.

Any type of exam and proof of performance can have an alternative mode of assessment. This includes exams that take place on site as well as in digital or hybrid form.

Examples of exam types where alternative examination methods can be arranged include:

- Oral exams
- Written exams
- Multiple-choice examinations
- Intermediate tests
- Presentations
- Active participation (e.g., discussions)
- Lab work
- Submissions (e.g., essays, final papers)
- Excursions, trips, museum visits, etc.

####

selected.title: Alternative mode of assessment
heading_path: Procedure for claiming an alternative mode of assessment
selected.text:
Exams can be modified in two ways to meet your needs based on your impairment:

- O[ption 1](#c639477): Direct communication and an agreement with the lecturer/examiner
- O[ption 2](#c639478): Submitting an application to the directorate of studies (SPL) [JP1] and presenting this application to the lecturer/examiner

In some cases, submitting a formal application ([option 2](#c639478)) can make more sense and/or be necessary. Please discuss your situation with your teachers/examiners and/or the Accessible Studying Team or the responsible directorate of studies. Please read the regulations by the individual directorates of studies (SPL) (links below) and check whether your SPL recommends one of the two options.

Consider which alternative mode of assessment might be most useful and effective for you to outweigh the disadvantages you experience.

- You are welcome to use the counselling offers of the Accessible Studying Team to find suitable (additional) alternative methods for your situation.

1. Discuss the necessary alternative modes of assessment – as soon as possible – with your lecturers/examiners directly and agree on the practical implementation.
2. Together, record what you agreed on in writing (e.g. via e-mail to ensure that all parties involved are well-informed and that the agreement is also transparent for others in the future).
3. If you are asked to provide evidence of your impairment because it is not obvious, please read the [information about the provision of evidence](https://studieren.univie.ac.at/barrierefrei-studieren/anpassungen-bei-pruefungenlven/nachweis-der-beeintraechtigung/) (in German).

selected.title: Alternative mode of assessment
selected.text:
If your application is granted or if you received a “letter of recommendation” (issued until February 2020), your teachers/examiners are not automatically notified and you are not automatically registered in the relevant university system.

A granted application merely confirms your right to the alternative mode of assessment and provides a solid basis for communication. You have to independently contact your lecturers/examiners in all your courses/exams and inform them that you require an alternative mode of assessment. This is also due to reasons of data protection. Furthermore, some impairments occur in episodes or phases. Therefore, affected students might not have the same needs at all times. An automated system could not take this into account.

Therefore, contact your lecturer or examiner as soon as possible to discuss your needs.

According to section 59, para. 1, item 12 of the [2002 Universities Act](https://www.ris.bka.gv.at/GeltendeFassung.wxe?Abfrage=Bundesnormen&Gesetzesnummer=20002128) (in German), degree programme students and non-degree programme students with an [impairment that directly affects their studies](/en/accessible-studies/) have the right to alternative modes of assessment.

In addition, the Statutes of the University of Vienna specify that there are [equal opportunities for people with a disability and/or a chronic illness](https://satzung.univie.ac.at/en/more-parts-of-the-statutes/equal-opportunities-for-people-with-disabilities-andor-chronic-illnesses/).

You can find the detailed legal texts on the [Accessible University of Vienna portal](https://barrierefrei.univie.ac.at/weiterfuehrend/rechtliches-und-nachweise/pruefungen/) (in German).

heading_path: Exam types and examples
When does which alternative mode make sense?
selected.text: The points listed below serve as examples only. Which different testing methods make sense for you personally must be assessed individually. Please note that different examination methods can always be guaranteed in coordination with the learning objectives of the respective curriculum and the feasibility. / - Attention- or concentration-disorders Extension of examination time; Examination in a separate room; extension of submission deadlines; using noise-cancelling headphones; Using a timer during exams; Possibility to use highlighters for written exams - Autism spectrum Written instead of oral exams; written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions; using noise-canceling headphones; Consideration of expression difficulties, avoidant eye contact, motor restlessness, strange-seeming behavior; Consideration of the difficulty regarding interpretation and one's own expression via facial expressions, gestures and speech melody - Anxiety/Panic Written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions; extension of examination time; Possibility to take a break during the exam; Compensation service instead of participation in excursions; Individual work instead of group work; extended absences; Permission to leave the room without comment if necessary; Possibility of completing the oral examination with as few people present as possible; Seat near the exit - Speech impairment Written instead of oral exams; Written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions - Reading and spelling difficulties Use of a laptop for written exams, extension of exam time for written exams; Possibility to use highlighters for written exams -

1. For your application, you require a [medical certificate](https://backend.univie.ac.at/index.php?id=109242) providing evidence of your impairment issued by a medical specialist. 2. Fill out the [application form](/fileadmin/user_upload/p_studieren/p_barrierefreistudieren/bf_docs/2026_antrag_abweichende_pruefungsmethoden.pdf) under the section "Personal Information" and point 2. No explanation of your situation is necessary in the application form! Team Barrierefrei can assist you with the application process. 3. Decide if you - wish to submit the application form to the Team Barrierefrei. On the application form, the Team Barrierefrei will note the relevant impairments in point 1. Afterward, you will receive the application form back and can submit it to the SPL, who is responsible for the final decision. - wish to submit the application form, along with the evidence of impairment, directly to the responsible [SPL](https://ufind.univie.ac.at/en/pvz_sub.html?id=99999904). - Please note: You have to submit a separate application for each degree programme (as well as for each extension curriculum (EC) and teaching subject) to the responsible SPL (for example, separate applications for the degree programmes in Chemistry, Sport and History). 4. Wait for possible enquiries and/or a notification e-mail informing you about the decision. 5. When your application has been granted, you can agree on the practical implementation of the alternative mode of assessment with your teachers/examiners. - Please contact them as soon as possible. - Together, record in writing what you and your teachers/examiners agreed on (e.g. via e-mail to ensure that all parties

[5] Alternative mode of assessment
If your application is granted or if you received a “letter of recommendation” (issued until February 2020), your teachers/examiners are not automatically notified and you are not automatically registered in the relevant university system.

A granted application merely confirms your right to the alternative mode of assessment and provides a solid basis for communication. You have to independently contact your lecturers/examiners in all your courses/exams and inform them that you require an alternative mode of assessment. This is also due to reasons of data protection. Furthermore, some impairments occur in episodes or phases. Therefore, affected students might not have the same needs at all times. An automated system could not take this into account.

Therefore, contact your lecturer or examiner as soon as possible to discuss your needs.

According to section 59, para. 1, item 12 of the [2002 Universities Act](https://www.ris.bka.gv.at/GeltendeFassung.wxe?Abfrage=Bundesnormen&Gesetzesnummer=20002128) (in German), degree programme students and non-degree programme students with an [impairment that directly affects their studies](/en/accessible-studies/) have the right to alternative modes of assessment.

In addition, the Statutes of the University of Vienna specify that there are [equal opportunities for people with a disability and/or a chronic illness](https://satzung.univie.ac.at/en/more-parts-of-the-statutes/equal-opportunities-for-people-with-disabilities-andor-chronic-illnesses/).

You can find the detailed legal texts on the [Accessible University of Vienna portal](https://barrierefrei.univie.ac.at/weiterfuehrend/rechtliches-und-nachweise/pruefungen/) (in German).

[6] Alternative mode of assessment — Exam types and examples > When does which alternative mode make sense?
The points listed below serve as examples only. Which different testing methods make sense for you personally must be assessed individually. Please note that different examination methods can always be guaranteed in coordination with the learning objectives of the respective curriculum and the feasibility. / - Attention- or concentration-disorders Extension of examination time; Examination in a separate room; extension of submission deadlines; using noise-cancelling headphones; Using a timer during exams; Possibility to use highlighters for written exams - Autism spectrum Written instead of oral exams; written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions; using noise-canceling headphones; Consideration of expression difficulties, avoidant eye contact, motor restlessness, strange-seeming behavior; Consideration of the difficulty regarding interpretation and one's own expression via facial expressions, gestures and speech melody - Anxiety/Panic Written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions; extension of examination time; Possibility to take a break during the exam; Compensation service instead of participation in excursions; Individual work instead of group work; extended absences; Permission to leave the room without comment if necessary; Possibility of completing the oral examination with as few people present as possible; Seat near the exit - Speech impairment Written instead of oral exams; Written alternative partial service instead of presentations/oral contributions - Reading and spelling difficulties Use of a laptop for written exams, extension of exam time for written exams; Possibility to use highlighters for written exams -

Question: What is an alternative mode of assessment and how do I apply for one?

generate.raw_response: An alternative mode of assessment is a modified exam or assessment method that allows students with impairments to complete exams without being disadvantaged [1]. To apply for one, you can either communicate directly with your lecturer/examiner and agree on an alternative mode [2], or submit an application to the directorate of studies (SPL) with required documents, such as a medical certificate and a filled-out application form [3][4].
